title: practical english usage - Part 350
content:
Are you still seeing that Henderson woman?

I'm seeing the dentist tomorrow.

'supervise', progressive forms are possible.

When see means 'meet', 'interview', 'talk to', 'go out with' or 'arrange', 3 'meet',   'arrange',     etc 576 vocabulary •         seem be crazy.. .)                              H not It seems crazy, but I think I'm in love with my bank manager, (     U seems to - The doctors have done the tests, and he definitely seems to be mentally ill.

She seems excited.

- The bus seems to be full.

always clear-cut, and both are often possible.) Compare: to be when we  are talking about subjective impressions. (The difference is not objective facts - things that seem definitely to be true. Seem is used without Seem is often followed by to be. V\Je prefer seem to be when we are talking about 2 seem      and   seem      to   be not You seem angry about something, (     You-seem-angrily-. ..) Seem is a linking verb (►   11); it is followed by adjectives, not adverbs.

used with  adjectives 1  linking     verb: 576 seem For if and whether, ►     261 .   There are similar differences between hear and listen (to), ►  181.

For other meanings of see (and progressive uses), ►     182,  1.   For other meanings of look, ►     "18.

For infinitives and  ing forms after these verbs, ►     ɪ  10.

Phone and see whether she's in.

I'm looking to see whether there's any food left, (     I'm looking whether not See if that suit still fits you. (     Look-ff-fhaf-sttit-...) not normally used in this way.

See can be followed by if/whether, in the sense of‘ find out’ . Look and watch are 6 see   if/whether We watched/saw a great film on TV last night.

You spend too much  time watching TV.

programmes and films.

Watch is normally used with TV; watch and see are both used to talk about TV 5  watch       TV watched Chaplin's-The-Great-Dictator'-?) not Have you ever seen Chaplin's 'The Great Dictator'? (     Have you ever He got into a fight yesterday afternoon while he was watching a football match, etc. Compare: We   often prefer see to talk about the whole of a performance, play, film, Watch is typically used to talk about experiences that are going on, in progress.

4 complete       experiences:        see I usually watch a football match on Saturday afternoon.

Watch that man - I want to know everything he does.

happen. We   watch things that change, move or develop.

Watch is like look at, but suggests that something is happening or going to 3  watch Word  Problems from A  to Z     Section 31 vocabulary •  576  seem For like and as. ►    513.   Appear is used in similar ways (►   374).

There seems to be some mistake.

There (►   605) can be a preparatory subject for seem to be.

It seemed as if the night was never going to end.

It seems that James and Alice have had a quarrel.

It can be a preparatory subject (►  268) for that- and as /-clauses after seem.

North Wales seems (like) a good place to go climbing, (     ... seems as a ...) We   can use like, but not as, after seem.

I can't seem to get anything right. (More formal: / seem not to be able to get Note the structure can't seem to .. .

He seems not to be at home, (formal) He doesn't seem to be at home.

more formal style not can go with the following infinitive. Compare: To  express a negative idea, we  most often use a negative form of seem; but in a The tax people seem to have made a mistake.

Perfect infinitives (►  89) are possible.

Anna seems to need a lot of attention.

Seem can be followed by the infinitives of other verbs besides be.

4 other     infinitives not It seems a pity, but I can't see you this weekend, (     It seems to be a pity...) The cup seemed almost doll's size in his hands.

She seems (to be) a nice girl.

subjective feelings, especially in British English.

However, to be can be dropped before noun phrases which  express more not I spoke to a man who seemed to be the boss, (     . .. who seemed-the-bos-s:) not (     ... it seemed some sort of large rat.) I looked through the binoculars: it seemed to be some sort of large rat.
